items.title: Centralize visibility of Kubernetes clusters across AWS Regions and accounts with EKS Dashboard
items.author: Micah Walter
items.content:
Today, we are announcing EKS Dashboard, a centralized display that enables cloud architects and cluster administrators to maintain organization-wide visibility across their Kubernetes clusters. With EKS Dashboard, customers can now monitor clusters deployed across different AWS Regions and accounts through a unified view, making it easier to track cluster inventory, assess compliance, and plan operational activities like version upgrades.

As organizations scale their Kubernetes deployments, they often run multiple clusters across different environments to enhance availability, ensure business continuity, or maintain data sovereignty. However, this distributed approach can make it challenging to maintain visibility and control, especially in decentralized setups spanning multiple Regions and accounts. Today, many customers resort to third-party tools for centralized cluster visibility, which adds complexity through identity and access setup, licensing costs, and maintenance overhead.

EKS Dashboard simplifies this experience by providing native dashboard capabilities within the AWS Console. The Dashboard provides insights into 3 different resources including clusters, managed node groups, and EKS add-ons, offering aggregated insights into cluster distribution by Region, account, version, support status, forecasted extended support EKS control plane costs, and cluster health metrics. Customers can drill down into speciﬁc data points with automatic ﬁltering, enabling them to quickly identify and focus on clusters requiring attention.

Customers can access the Dashboard in EKS console through AWS Organizations’ management and delegated administrator accounts. The setup process is straightforward and includes simply enabling trusted access as a one-time setup in the Amazon EKS console’s organizations settings page. Trusted access is available from the page. Enabling trusted access will allow the management account to view the Dashboard. For more information on setup and configuration, see the official AWS Documentation.

A quick tour of EKS Dashboard

The dashboard provides both graphical, tabular, and map views of your Kubernetes clusters, with advanced filtering, and search capabilities. You can also export data for further analysis or custom reporting.

EKS Dashboard overview with key info about your clusters.

There is a wide variety of available widgets to help visualize your clusters.

You can visualize your managed node groups by instance type distribution, launch templates, AMI versions, and more

There is even a map view where you can see all of your clusters across the globe.

EKS Dashboard isn’t limited to just Amazon EKS clusters; it can also provide visibility into connected Kubernetes clusters running on-premises or on other cloud providers. While connected clusters may have limited data fidelity compared to native Amazon EKS clusters, this capability enables truly unified visibility for organizations running hybrid or multi-cloud environments.

EKS Dashboard is available today in the US East (N. Virginia) Region and is able to aggregate data from all commercial AWS Regions. There is no additional charge for using the EKS Dashboard. To learn more, visit the Amazon EKS documentation.

This new capability demonstrates our continued commitment to simplifying Kubernetes operations for our customers, enabling them to focus on building and scaling their applications rather than managing infrastructure. We’re excited to see how customers use EKS Dashboard to enhance their Kubernetes operations.

— Micah;

items.title: Amazon Q Developer CLI supports image inputs in your terminal
items.author: Keerthi Sreenivas Konjety
items.content:
In this post I will explore how the image support feature in Amazon Q Developer Command Line Interface (CLI) transforms development workflows. Q Developer CLI recently added image support, expanding its capabilities to process visual information and enhancing developer productivity. This new feature allows developers to interact with diagrams, architecture blueprints, and other visual assets directly through the command line.

Modern software development increasingly relies on visual representations to communicate ideas. For example, architecture diagrams illustrate system components and their interactions, while entity-relationship diagrams map out database structures. Translating visual assets into working code is usually a manual, error-prone process of interpretation and implementation.

The new image support in Q Developer CLI bridges this gap by allowing developers to provide images directly to the Q Developer CLI agent for analysis. I’m excited to use this feature to transform my architecture diagrams from scrappy, hand-drawn ideas to polished design documents, and then to infrastructure as code. I look forward to applying it in various use cases, whether I’m getting started on a new project or streamlining my daily workflows.

At the time of launch, the Q Developer CLI supports JPEG, PNG, WEBP, and GIF image formats along with the ability to upload 10 images per request. You must use the latest version (1.10.0 or above) of Q developer CLI to enjoy the image support feature in Q Developer CLI. Use this guide to upgrade or install the latest version.

I will use the following four scenarios as examples to demonstrate the benefit of image support for Q Developer CLI.

Use-case 1: Generating infrastructure as code from an architecture diagram

The following diagram depicts an application that resizes images. It includes a source Amazon S3 bucket into which a user uploads an image, and an AWS Lambda function that resizes the image and stores it in a destination S3 Bucket. I can now convert architecture diagrams to code using Q Developer CLI.

Architecture for an image resizing application

In the following screenshot, I asked the Q Developer CLI to “Please provide me with a reference terraform template using best practices”. Note that dragging and dropping the image into the CLI will add the path to your prompt.

CLI with Terraform code generated by Q Developer

The prior image shows a portion of the response that Q Developer CLI has generated.

Q Developer responds with the terraform template required to get started with building the image resizing application. Q Developer CLI analyzed the image, identified the components and their relationships, and generated the corresponding Terraform code. While not shown in the image, the response included the Lambda function’s code in Python and the IAM permissions needed for the Lambda function.

Previously, transforming this diagram into infrastructure as code would require me to manually interpret each component and write the corresponding configuration. With image support, I can now automate much of this process and refine the generated code through a conversation with Q Developer. I can then have a conversation with Q Developer to refine the generated code, ask questions about specific implementation details, or request modifications based on additional requirements and output the code to a .tf file.

Use-case 2: Converting ER diagrams to database schemas

For our second scenario, let’s consider a use case where I’m a part of a data modeling team developing a course management software for universities. I have created an entity-relationship (ER) diagram for their core data structures. I can now use Q developer to help me convert the ER diagram to SQL.

Course management Entity Relationship Diagram

In the following screenshot, I asked the Q Developer CLI to use the ER diagram to create the database schema.

CLI with user prompt and SQL generated by Q Developer

CLI with SQL generated by Q Developer

The prior image shows the response the that Q Developer CLI generated.

Q Developer analyzed the diagram, identified entities, attributes, and relationships, then generated the appropriate SQL code for creating the database schema.

After Q Developer produces the results, I can refine this schema through a conversation with Q Developer by requesting changes to string lengths, indexes, etc., or requesting explanations of design decisions.

Use-case 3: Converting a hand drawn image to a design document

Consider a scenario where I have brainstormed an idea on paper and I would like to share this with my team. In the following image, I have hand drawn the order flow for a website. When the website user orders books from the website, the application updates inventory, then calls the payment and delivery actions. I can now use the Q Developer CLI to draft documentation from the hand drawn idea.

Hand drawn order flow for a website

In the following example, I asked Q Developer to write a design document using this image as a reference.

CLI with user prompt and response generated by Q Developer

The above screenshot shows that, Q Developer first read the image and understood the content from the hand drawn diagram image.

CLI with the response generated by Q Developer

The prior screen shot is a portion of the response that Q Developer CLI has generated.

Q Developer converted the idea into a design document including system architecture, process flow, data model, functional requirements, and technical requirements. I can also ask Q Developer to output the context to a .md file. This reduces the amount of time going from idea to execution and streamlines document writing.

Use-case 4: Building a UI mockup/wireframe from a screen shot

Let’s say, I want to get started with building a User Interface (UI) from my design document from use-case 3. I can provide a reference image to Q Developer for generating initial wireframes for my UI.

Sample book sales website home page

In this example, I asked Q Developer to help generate a front-end for a new website in Vue.js

CLI with the user prompt and response generated by Q Developer

CLI with Vue.js code generated by Q Developer

The prior image shows a portion of the Vue.js code generated by the Q Developer CLI to re-produce the front-end of the website in the screenshot. Once I verify the code, I can then ask Q Developer CLI to create these files locally.

This approach reduces the error-prone aspects of wireframe creation, allowing me to focus on creative design decisions instead of repetitive setup tasks. In this way, I can accelerate development cycles, ensure consistency across components, and provide a foundation that can be easily customized to meet specific project requirements.

Additional possibilities:

Apart from the prior examples, Q Developer CLI can analyze many types of images, including:

  • Flow charts and process diagrams
  • Class diagrams for object-oriented design
  • Network topology diagrams
  • Screenshots of error messages or application states

This versatility makes Q Developer CLI a powerful tool for various development workflows.

The addition of image support to Amazon Q Developer CLI represents a significant step forward in bridging the gap between visual and textual representations in software development. By allowing me to work with diagrams and other visual assets directly from the command line, Amazon Q Developer improves my efficiency in translating design into implementation, reducing errors and accelerating development cycles. I encourage you to explore this new capability and discover how it can enhance your development workflow.

To learn more about Q Developer and its capabilities, visit the documentation.

Keerthi Sreenivas Konjety

Keerthi Sreenivas Konjety is a Specialist Solutions Architect for Amazon Q Developer, with over 3.5 years of experience in AI, ML and Data Engineering. Her expertise lies in enabling developer productivity for AWS customers. Outside work, she enjoys photography and AI content creation.

items.title: Configure System Integrity Protection (SIP) on Amazon EC2 Mac instances
items.author: Sébastien Stormacq
items.content:
I’m pleased to announce developers can now programmatically disable Apple System Integrity Protection (SIP) on their Amazon EC2 Mac instances. System Integrity Protection (SIP), also known as rootless, is a security feature introduced by Apple in OS X El Capitan (2015, version 10.11). It’s designed to protect the system from potentially harmful software by restricting the power of the root user account. SIP is enabled by default on macOS.

SIP safeguards the system by preventing modification of protected files and folders, restricting access to system-owned files and directories, and blocking unauthorized software from selecting a startup disk. The primary goal of SIP is to address the security risk linked to unrestricted root access, which could potentially allow malware to gain full control of a device with just one password or vulnerability. By implementing this protection, Apple aims to ensure a higher level of security for macOS users, especially considering that many users operate on administrative accounts with weak or no passwords.

While SIP provides excellent protection against malware for everyday use, developers might occasionally need to temporarily disable it for development and testing purposes. For instance, when creating a new device driver or system extension, disabling SIP is necessary to install and test the code. Additionally, SIP might block access to certain system settings required for your software to function properly. Temporarily disabling SIP grants you the necessary permissions to fine-tune programs for macOS. However, it’s crucial to remember that this is akin to briefly disabling the vault door for authorized maintenance, not leaving it permanently open.

Until today, you had to operate with the standard SIP settings on EC2 Mac instances. The physical access requirement and the need to boot in recovery mode made integrating SIP with the Amazon EC2 control plane and EC2 API challenging. But that’s no longer the case! You can now disable and re-enable SIP at will on your Amazon EC2 Mac instances. Let me show you how.

Imagine I have an Amazon EC2 Mac instance started. It’s a instance, running on an Apple silicon M2 processor. Disabling or enabling SIP is as straightforward as calling a new EC2 API: CreateMacSystemIntegrityProtectionModificationTask. This API is asynchronous; it starts the process of changing the SIP status on your instance. You can monitor progress using another new EC2 API: DescribeMacModificationTasks. All I need to know is the instance ID of the machine I want to work with.

On Apple silicon based EC2 Mac instances and more recent type of machines, before calling the new EC2 API, I must set the user password and enable secure token for that user on macOS. This requires connecting to the machine and typing two commands in the terminal.

# on the target EC2 Mac instance
# Set a password for the ec2-user user
~ % sudo /usr/bin/dscl . -passwd /Users/ec2-user
New Password: (MyNewPassw0rd)

# Enable secure token, with the same password, for the ec2-user
# old password is the one you just set with dscl
~ % sysadminctl -newPassword MyNewPassw0rd -oldPassword MyNewPassw0rd
2025-03-05 13:16:57.261 sysadminctl[3993:3033024] Attempting to change password for ec2-user…
2025-03-05 13:16:58.690 sysadminctl[3993:3033024] SecKeychainCopyLogin returned -25294
2025-03-05 13:16:58.690 sysadminctl[3993:3033024] Failed to update keychain password (-25294)
2025-03-05 13:16:58.690 sysadminctl[3993:3033024] - Done

# The error about the KeyChain is expected. I never connected with the GUI on this machine, so the Login keychain does not exist
# you can ignore this error.  The command below shows the list of keychains active in this session
~ % security list
    "/Library/Keychains/System.keychain"

# Verify that the secure token is ENABLED
~ % sysadminctl -secureTokenStatus ec2-user
2025-03-05 13:18:12.456 sysadminctl[4017:3033614] Secure token is ENABLED for user ec2-user

I don’t need to connect to the machine to toggle the SIP status. I only need to know its instance ID. I open a terminal on my laptop and use the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI) to retrieve the Amazon EC2 Mac instance ID.

 aws ec2 describe-instances \
         --query "Reservations[].Instances[?InstanceType == 'mac2-m2.metal' ].InstanceId" \
         --output text

i-012a5de8da47bdff7

Now, still from the terminal on my laptop, I disable SIP with the create-mac-system-integrity-protection-modification-task command:

echo '{"rootVolumeUsername":"ec2-user","rootVolumePassword":"MyNewPassw0rd"}' > tmpCredentials
aws ec2 create-mac-system-integrity-protection-modification-task \
--instance-id "i-012a5de8da47bdff7" \
--mac-credentials fileb://./tmpCredentials \
--mac-system-integrity-protection-status "disabled" && rm tmpCredentials

{
    "macModificationTask": {
        "instanceId": "i-012a5de8da47bdff7",
        "macModificationTaskId": "macmodification-06a4bb89b394ac6d6",
        "macSystemIntegrityProtectionConfig": {},
        "startTime": "2025-03-14T14:15:06Z",
        "taskState": "pending",
        "taskType": "sip-modification"
    }
}

After the task is started, I can check its status with the aws ec2 describe-mac-modification-tasks command.

{
    "macModificationTasks": [
        {
            "instanceId": "i-012a5de8da47bdff7",
            "macModificationTaskId": "macmodification-06a4bb89b394ac6d6",
            "macSystemIntegrityProtectionConfig": {
                "debuggingRestrictions": "",
                "dTraceRestrictions": "",
                "filesystemProtections": "",
                "kextSigning": "",
                "nvramProtections": "",
                "status": "disabled"
            },
            "startTime": "2025-03-14T14:15:06Z",
            "tags": [],
            "taskState": "in-progress",
            "taskType": "sip-modification"
        },
...

The instance initiates the process and a series of reboots, during which it becomes unreachable. This process can take 60–90 minutes to complete. After that, when I see the status in the console becoming available again, I connect to the machine through SSH or EC2 Instance Connect, as usual.

➜  ~ ssh ec2-user@54.99.9.99
Warning: Permanently added '54.99.9.99' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
Last login: Mon Feb 26 08:52:42 2024 from 1.1.1.1

    ┌───┬──┐   __|  __|_  )
    │ ╷╭╯╷ │   _|  (     /
    │  └╮  │  ___|\___|___|
    │ ╰─┼╯ │  Amazon EC2
    └───┴──┘  macOS Sonoma 14.3.1

➜  ~ uname -a
Darwin Mac-mini.local 23.3.0 Darwin Kernel Version 23.3.0: Wed Dec 20 21:30:27 PST 2023; root:xnu-10002.81.5~7/RELEASE_ARM64_T8103 arm64

➜ ~ csrutil --status 
System Integrity Protection status: disabled.

Disabling SIP should be approached with caution because it opens up the system to potential security risks. However, as I mentioned in the introduction of this post, you might need to disable SIP when developing device drivers or kernel extensions for macOS. Some older applications might also not function correctly when SIP is enabled.

Disabling SIP is also required to turn off Spotlight indexing. Spotlight can help you quickly find apps, documents, emails and other items on your Mac. It’s very convenient on desktop machines, but not so much on a server. When there is no need to index your documents as they change, turning off Spotlight will release some CPU cycles and disk I/O.

There are a couple of additional things to know about disabling SIP on Amazon EC2 Mac:

  • On Apple silicon, the setting is volume based. So if you replace the root volume, you need to disable SIP again. On Intel, the setting is Mac host based, so if you replace the root volume, SIP will still be disabled.
  • After disabling SIP, it will be enabled again if you stop and start the instance. Rebooting an instance doesn’t change its SIP status.
  • SIP status isn’t transferable between EBS volumes. This means SIP will be disabled again after you restore an instance from an EBS snapshot or if you create an AMI from an instance where SIP is enabled.
— seb

(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

items.title: SETTING UP AN MLOPS WORKFLOW WITH DAGSHUB AND MLFLOW
items.author: Hasan Tuğra Aykaç
items.content:
Developing and managing machine learning projects can be a complex process for modern data science teams. MLOps has emerged as a powerful approach to streamline this process and automate the workflow from model development to deployment. In this blog post, we will explore step-by-step how to build an effective MLOps workflow using tools like and . By combining Dagshub’s data and model management capabilities with MLflow’s experiment tracking and deployment features, you can make your projects more efficient and scalable. Get ready to dive into the world of MLOps and discover how to optimize your workflow with these powerful tools.

Dagshub is a platform that integrates with GitHub, so we need to create a repository on GitHub. You can optionally add a .gitignore and README. I have created a public repository named , and I will proceed with this repository. Now, we clone the repository to our computer, create a Conda environment, and install the libraries listed in the requirements.txt file. If everything is ready, we can start coding and connect our repository to Dagshub.

You need to have a Dagshub account; you can log in using your GitHub account or Google account. After logging in, you will be greeted by the Dagshub dashboard.

Click on the “Create +” button in the top right corner.

In the opened window, click on the “Connect a repository” menu. After clicked you will see two different options: GitHub and Other. Select the GitHub option here.

You will see a section like the one in the image above. Select the repository you created and click the Save button.

Finally, we are greeted by a section like this on Dagshub. This is the final part related to DagsHub. Now, let’s tackle the coding. For this, I am creating a file named main.py, and I first need to import the necessary libraries.

import osimport warningsimport logging
from urllib.parse import urlparse
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
import mlflowimport dagshub
# Dagshub integrationdagshub.init(repo_owner='hasan.asus1999', repo_name='MLOPS-Demo', mlflow=True)
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.WARN)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

The line dagshub.init(repo_owner=’hasan.asus1999', repo_name=’MLOPS-Demo’, mlflow=True) is very important, and this code block is provided by DagsHub.

On the Dagshub dashboard, click the “Remote” button, navigate to the “Experiments” menu, and select the marked code line under “Using MLflow tracking.”

def eval_classification_metrics(actual, pred):    acc = accuracy_score(actual, pred)
    prec = precision_score(actual, pred, average='weighted')
    rec = recall_score(actual, pred, average='weighted')
    f1 = f1_score(actual, pred, average='weighted')
    return acc, prec, rec, f1

By writing the eval_classification_metrics function, we will use the classification metrics and make comparisons in MLflow.

if __name__ == "__main__":    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")    csv_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uiuc-cse/data-fa14/gh-pages/data/iris.csv"        data = pd.read_csv(csv_url)        logger.exception("Unable to download CSV. Error: %s", e)
    # separate dependent and independent variables
    X = data.drop("species", axis=1)
    # training/testing distinction
    X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.25, random_state=42)    n_estimators = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 100
    max_depth = int(sys.argv[2]) if len(sys.argv) > 2 else None
        clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=n_estimators, max_depth=max_depth, random_state=42)
        clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
        y_pred = clf.predict(X_test)
        acc, prec, rec, f1 = eval_classification_metrics(y_test, y_pred)
        print(f"Random Forest (n_estimators={n_estimators}, max_depth={max_depth}):")
        print(f"  Accuracy: {acc}")
        print(f"  Precision: {prec}")
        print(f"  Recall: {rec}")
        print(f"  F1 Score: {f1}")
        mlflow.log_param("n_estimators", n_estimators)
        mlflow.log_param("max_depth", max_depth)
        mlflow.log_metric("accuracy", acc)
        mlflow.log_metric("precision", prec)
        mlflow.log_metric("recall", rec)
        mlflow.log_metric("f1_score", f1)        remote_server_uri = "https://dagshub.com/hasan.asus1999/MLflow-Test.mlflow"
        mlflow.set_tracking_uri(remote_server_uri)
        tracking_url_type_store = urlparse(mlflow.get_tracking_uri()).scheme
        if tracking_url_type_store != "file":
            mlflow.sklearn.log_model(clf, "model", registered_model_name="RandomForestIrisModel")            mlflow.sklearn.log_model(clf, "model")

I found it appropriate to use the Iris dataset for this study because it is a fundamental dataset for classification algorithms. This code block, csv_url = “https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uiuc-cse/data-fa14/gh-pages/data/iris.csv" will help me use the Iris dataset, meaning I won’t need to download it from Kaggle or any other source. If you want, you can find more detailed information about the Iris dataset here. Another important point is the Dagshub URI, which is this line of code: remote_server_uri = “https://dagshub.com/hasan.asus1999/MLflow-Test.mlflow". This line is necessary for us to connect to MLflow. So how can we find this in the DagsHub dashboard?

On the Dagshub dashboard, click the “Remote” button, navigate to the “Experiments” menu, and select the marked code line under “MLflow Tracking remote”.

Now everything is ready. Based on the image above, let’s click on the “Go to mlflow UI” button and go to MLflow.

We are greeted by an empty screen; nothing is visible because I haven’t run the relevant Python code. To run my Python code, execute it by typing or .

If there are no errors in your DagsHub connections and code, you should see an output like this in the console.

Let’s explain the output line by line. Accessing as hasan.asus1999 means you’re authenticated and accessing your correctly. Initialized MLflow to track repo “hasan.asus1999/MLOPS-Demo” means MLflow has been successfully initialized to log all training runs, metrics, and models to your named MLOPS-Demo. Random Forest (n_estimators=100, max_depth=None) indicates the model was trained using the default hyperparameters. If I do not give parameter values ​​to my RandomForest algorithm, my default n_estimators value will be 100 and my max_depth value will be None. We will try it by giving parameters in another example and compare it in MLflow.

# Hyperparametersn_estimators = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 100
max_depth = int(sys.argv[2]) if len(sys.argv) > 2 else None

Do you remember the code line from above? That code line allows us to use the default values, but we will break this when we rerun our application shortly.

  Accuracy: 1.0  Precision: 1.0  F1 Score: 1.0

These metrics show perfect scores (1.0), which means the model performed flawlessly on the test data. This is expected for the Iris dataset, which is small, clean, and well-balanced.

Successfully registered model ‘RandomForestIrisModel’ is trained model was registered successfully in the MLflow Model Registry under the name RandomForestIrisModel.

Created version ‘1’ of model ‘RandomForestIrisModel’. This model is stored as version 1. If you train and log the model again (with different parameters), MLflow will create version 2, 3, etc.

Now let’s refresh our MLflow dashboard page and make sure we see something like this.

As you can see, everything is fine. In the image above, when we click on the Run Name field next to the orange icon, we can access details such as parameters and metrics.

It matches perfectly with the values in our console; this is awesome!

Now let’s try different parameters for our RandomForest algorithm. I will run my application this time as follows: This means the n_estimators value will be 150 and the max_depth value will be 5.

Now let’s refresh our MLflow dashboard and make sure we can see this image.

As you can see we now have two different models. We can visually compare two models by selecting the checkboxes next to the models and clicking the “Compare” button.

You can visualize by selecting your parameters and metrics. I tried selecting all parameters and metrics; you can try being more specific.

items.title: Tech stack 101 : Ep-29 : How to optimize your database queries ?
items.author: Nagarjun Gopalaswamy
items.content:
Tech stack 101 : Ep-29 : How to optimize your database queries ?

Optimizing a slow-running database query involves identifying and addressing performance bottlenecks in the query execution plan, indexing strategy, database configuration, and data model. Here are some steps you can take to optimize a slow database query

Start by identifying the specific query or queries that are running slow. Use database monitoring tools, query logs, or profiling tools to pinpoint the queries with the longest execution times.

This below query retrieves all orders placed after January 1, 2022 and its fetching all the columns.

SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2022-01-01';
Analyze the Execution Plan:

Use database query execution analysis tools or built-in database features (e.g., EXPLAIN in SQL databases) to examine the query execution plan. Look for inefficient operations such as full table scans, inefficient joins, or missing indexes.

The execution plan shows that a full table scan is being performed on the orders table, which could indicate a potential performance issue for large datasets.

EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2022-01-01';

Once you have identified the inefficient parts of the query execution plan, optimize the query to improve performance. This may involve rewriting the query to use more efficient SQL constructs, adding hints to influence the query optimizer, or restructuring the query to minimize data movement and processing.

By adding an ORDER BY clause, the query optimizer may choose to use an index to fulfill the query, resulting in improved performance.

SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2022-01-01' ORDER BY order_date;
Review Indexing Strategy:

Evaluate the indexing strategy for the tables involved in the query. Ensure that appropriate indexes are in place to support the query’s filtering, sorting, and joining operations. Consider adding, modifying, or dropping indexes as needed to improve query performance.

Adding an index on the order_date column can speed up queries that filter or sort by this column.

CREATE INDEX idx_order_date ON orders(order_date);

Update table and index statistics to ensure that the query optimizer has accurate information about the data distribution and cardinality. Outdated statistics can lead to suboptimal query plans and performance degradation.

Updating table statistics ensures that the query optimizer has accurate information about the data distribution, which can improve query planning.

Optimize Database Configuration:

Review the database configuration settings such as memory allocation, disk I/O settings, and parallelism settings. Adjust these settings based on the workload characteristics and hardware resources available to the database server.

Adjusting the InnoDB buffer pool size can improve the caching of frequently accessed data, leading to better query performance.

SET GLOBAL innodb_buffer_pool_size = 2G;
Partitioning and Sharding:

Consider partitioning or sharding large tables to distribute data across multiple physical or logical partitions. This can improve query performance by reducing the amount of data that needs to be scanned or processed for a given query.

Partitioning the orders table by year can improve query performance by limiting the amount of data that needs to be scanned for each query.

ALTER TABLE orders PARTITION BY RANGE (YEAR(order_date)) (    PARTITION p1 VALUES LESS THAN (2020),
    PARTITION p2 VALUES LESS THAN (2021),
    PARTITION p3 VALUES LESS THAN (2022),
    PARTITION p4 VALUES LESS THAN MAXVALUE
);
Optimize Application Code:

Review the application code that generates and executes the query. Look for opportunities to optimize the code by reducing the number of database round-trips, minimizing data fetching, and optimizing data processing logic.

By fetching only the required columns instead of using SELECT *, the amount of data transferred between the database server and the application can be minimized, improving performance.

After implementing optimizations, monitor the query performance to ensure that the changes have the desired effect. Iterate on the optimization process as needed, making further adjustments based on performance metrics and user feedback.

Here are some best practices to write SQL queries aimed at good performace :

  1. Proper indexing is crucial for efficient query execution. Identify the columns frequently used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses, and create indexes on those columns. However, avoid over-indexing, as it can degrade insert and update performance.
  2. : Retrieve only the necessary columns and rows by specifying them explicitly in the SELECT statement. Avoid using SELECT * and fetch only the required data to minimize network and disk I/O overhead.
  3. : Use JOINs judiciously and ensure that JOIN conditions are optimized. Prefer INNER JOIN over OUTER JOIN when possible, and use appropriate JOIN strategies (e.g., nested loops, hash joins) based on the data distribution and query requirements.
  4. Minimize the use of subqueries, especially correlated subqueries, as they can be inefficient. Consider rewriting subqueries as JOINs or using common table expressions (CTEs) to improve query performance.
  5. When using subqueries, prefer EXISTS and IN clauses over NOT EXISTS and NOT IN for better performance. EXISTS and IN can be optimized by the database engine more efficiently than NOT EXISTS and NOT IN.
  6. Avoid Functions in WHERE Clause: Avoid using functions in the WHERE clause, as they can prevent the use of indexes and lead to full table scans. If possible, precompute or denormalize data to avoid the need for function calls in queries.
  7. Minimize Data Manipulation: Minimize data manipulation operations (e.g., sorting, grouping, aggregating) in the database layer. Consider offloading complex calculations and data processing to application code or dedicated data processing tools.
  8. Use Parameterized Queries: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements to prevent SQL injection attacks and improve query plan caching. Parameterized queries allow the database engine to reuse query plans, resulting in better performance for repeated executions of the same query with different parameter values.
  9. Avoid using cursors and iterative processing in SQL queries whenever possible, as they can be slow and resource-intensive. Instead, use set-based operations and bulk processing techniques to perform data manipulation efficiently.
  10. Monitor and Tune Regularly: Monitor query performance using database monitoring tools and query execution plans. Identify and address performance bottlenecks proactively by tuning indexes, optimizing queries, and adjusting database configuration settings as needed.

items.title: Tech stack 101 : Ep-04 AWS database services and its use cases
items.author: Nagarjun Gopalaswamy
items.content:
Tech stack 101 : Ep-04 Types of Databases and different database services offered by AWS

A brief introduction to a few types of databases in the industry today.

overall the data can be categorized into 3 kinds : 1. Structured data with schema.

2. Semi structured data like documents of xml which is hierarchical

3. Unstructured data like logs which are just blobs of data.

Depending on the type of data being dealt with, we choose the type of database to be used to store that data. Here are a few different types of database in the market today and its AWS flavors are discussed below.

Row Store (Relational Database):
  • In a row store database, data is stored and retrieved row by row. This means that all columns of a single row are stored together.
  • It follows the traditional relational database model where data is organized into tables with rows and columns.
  • Examples of relational databases include MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle Database, SQL Server, etc.
  • Suitable for transactional systems where the primary operation involves querying a small number of rows with many columns.
  • In a column store database, data is stored and retrieved column by column. This means that all values of a single column are stored together.
  • It stores each column of data separately, which can provide significant performance benefits for certain types of queries, especially those that involve aggregations or analytics.
  • Column stores are often used for data warehousing and analytical workloads where queries typically aggregate large amounts of data across many rows but only a few columns.
  • Examples of column store databases include Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Apache Cassandra, Vertica, etc.

A Graph Database is a type of NoSQL database that uses graph structures for semantic queries with nodes, edges, and properties to represent and store data. Here’s a brief overview along with some common use cases and AWS services related to graph databases:

  • Graph databases organize data into nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties (attributes of nodes and edges).
  • : Graph databases typically use graph query languages like Cypher (used in Neo4j) or Gremlin (used in Apache TinkerPop) to perform graph traversals and pattern matching.
  • : Graph databases excel at handling complex relationships and traversing connections between data points efficiently, making them suitable for scenarios with highly interconnected data.
  1. Social Networks: Graph databases are well-suited for modeling social network relationships between users, friends, followers, and interests.
  2. Recommendation Engines: They can power recommendation systems by analyzing user behavior and preferences, identifying patterns, and suggesting relevant content or products.
  3. Fraud Detection: Graph databases can detect fraudulent activities by analyzing patterns and connections between entities such as accounts, transactions, and IP addresses.

A Document Database, also known as a Document Store or Document-Oriented Database, is a type of NoSQL database that stores, retrieves, and manages data in the form of semi-structured documents, typically encoded in formats like JSON, BSON, or XML. Here’s an overview of Document Databases and when to use them:

  1. Flexible Schema: Document databases offer a flexible schema model, allowing documents within a collection to have varying structures and fields. This flexibility makes it easy to evolve the data model over time without the need for rigid schemas.
  2. Document Model: Data is stored in the form of documents, which are self-descriptive, hierarchical structures containing key-value pairs or nested objects. Each document can represent a single entity or record, and related documents can be nested or linked together.
  3. Rich Querying: Document databases support rich querying capabilities, allowing developers to perform complex queries, filtering, and indexing based on document contents, nested fields, and array elements.
  4. Horizontal Scalability: Many document databases are designed to scale horizontally across distributed clusters of nodes, enabling seamless scaling to accommodate growing data volumes and increasing query loads.
  5. High Performance: Document databases often provide fast read and write performance by optimizing data storage and retrieval mechanisms, leveraging indexes, and caching frequently accessed documents.
  6. Schema Evolution: With a schema-less or schema-on-read approach, document databases allow developers to insert, update, and query data without predefined schemas, making them suitable for agile development and iterative prototyping.

When to Use Document Databases:

  1. Semi-Structured Data: Document databases are ideal for managing semi-structured or unstructured data where the schema may vary across different documents or collections, such as user profiles, product catalogs, blog posts, and content management systems.
  2. Agile Development: They are well-suited for agile development environments where requirements are subject to change, as they allow developers to iterate quickly and adapt data models on the fly without schema migrations.
  3. Hierarchical Data: Document databases excel at storing hierarchical or nested data structures, such as JSON or XML documents, where relationships between entities are naturally represented as nested objects or arrays.

A Time-Series Database (TSDB) is a type of database optimized for handling time-stamped or time-series data, where each data point is associated with a timestamp indicating when it was recorded. Here’s an introduction to TSDBs along with some key characteristics and use cases:

  1. : TSDBs are designed to efficiently store, retrieve, and analyze data points that are indexed by time.
  2. : They are optimized for ingesting large volumes of data points in real-time or at high frequencies, making them suitable for applications that generate continuous streams of data.
  3. Efficient Time-Based Queries: TSDBs provide fast querying capabilities to retrieve data based on time ranges, enabling time-based aggregations, filtering, and downsampling.
  4. Compression and Downsampling: To handle large datasets efficiently, TSDBs often support compression techniques and downsampling algorithms to reduce storage requirements without sacrificing data fidelity.
  5. : TSDBs are designed to scale horizontally to accommodate growing data volumes and increasing query loads, allowing for distributed storage and processing.
  1. Monitoring and Observability: TSDBs are commonly used for collecting and analyzing metrics, logs, and events from various sources such as IT infrastructure, applications, sensors, and IoT devices. They enable real-time monitoring, anomaly detection, and troubleshooting.
  2. Financial Analytics: TSDBs are utilized in financial services for storing and analyzing time-series data related to stock prices, market trends, trading volumes, and transaction histories. They support backtesting, risk analysis, and algorithmic trading strategies.

Here’s a brief overview of each AWS database service and its use case:

  1. : A MySQL and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database built for the cloud, offering high performance and scalability. Use cases include OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) workloads, e-commerce applications, and SaaS platforms.
  2. : A fully managed data warehousing service that allows you to run complex analytical queries on large datasets. Ideal for data warehousing, business intelligence, and analytics applications.
  3. : A fully managed NoSQL database service that provides single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. Best suited for applications requiring low-latency data access, such as gaming, ad tech, and real-time bidding platforms.
  4. : A fully managed in-memory caching service compatible with Redis and Memcached, enabling you to deploy and manage distributed caches in the cloud. Commonly used to improve application performance by caching frequently accessed data.
  5. (for Apache Cassandra): A scalable, highly available, and fully managed Cassandra-compatible database service. Ideal for applications that require a wide-column store with high availability, such as IoT data processing and real-time analytics.
  6. : A fully managed time-series database service designed for IoT, telemetry, and operational applications. It can efficiently store and analyze time-series data at scale, making it suitable for monitoring, IoT, and industrial applications.
  7. Amazon Quantum Ledger Database (QLDB): A fully managed ledger database service that provides a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log. Use cases include auditing, compliance, and supply chain tracking where data integrity and immutability are critical.
  8. A fully managed graph database service that allows you to build and run graph applications. Ideal for applications requiring highly connected data models, such as social networks, recommendation engines, and fraud detection.
  9. : A fully managed MongoDB-compatible document database service that provides scalability, performance, and availability. Best suited for applications that require the flexibility of a document database with the reliability and scalability of AWS.
  10. Amazon OpenSearch Service: A fully managed service that makes it easy to deploy, operate, and scale Elasticsearch for log analytics, full-text search, and real-time application monitoring. Use cases include log analytics, search engines, and monitoring solutions
  11. Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service): A scalable object storage service designed for data storage and retrieval. While not a database service, S3 is commonly used for storing large volumes of unstructured data, such as images, videos, and backups.

Each of these services caters to different data storage and processing requirements, so the choice depends on factors such as data volume, access patterns, query complexity, and scalability needs.

items.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (7/10): Unit- & Integration Testing
items.author: Asbjorn Bering
items.content:
Building an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), Terraform

In the previous articles we’ve focused on developing our microservice, following the Repository Pattern. We have achieved loose coupling wrt. database implementation, making it possible to easily swap out databases down the line. Another great thing about loose coupling, is when it comes to writing unit tests for our service, as you shall see in this article.

In addition to unit testing, we’ll also focus on integration testing our microservice in isolation — not distributed integration testing across multiple microservices. Since this series is about building an enterprise-grade microservice, we’ll focus on isolated tests; distributed tests are out of scope for now.

We’re now adding our testsfolder. For reference, here’s the updated project structure. In this article we are going through everything that happens in our tests folder, and how we can extend our Dockerfile.dev to run tests automatically.

├── Dockerfile.dev├── Dockerfile.prod├── docker-compose.yaml├── src│   ├── db│   │   ├── db_context.py│   │   └── settings.py│   ├── exceptions.py
│   ├── logging_config.py
│   ├── main.py│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── correlation_context.py
│   │   └── correlation_id_middleware.py│   │   ├── implementations
│   │   │   ├── AWS_DynamoDB
│   │   │   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   │   │   ├── awsdynamodb_UserRepository.py
│   │   │   │   └── utils.py│   │   │   └── PostgreSQL
│   │   │       ├── __init__.py
│   │   │       ├── debezium_config.py
│   │   │       ├── models
│   │   │       │   ├── ORM_User.py
│   │   │       │   └── __init__.py
│   │   │       └── postgres_UserRepository.py│   │       ├── __init__.py
│   │       └── interface_UserRepository.py
│   ├── routes
│   │   ├── UserController.py
│   │   └── __init__.py│   │   ├── UserSchemas.py│   └── service
│       ├── UserService.py
│       ├── __init__.py├── tests│   ├── integration│   │   ├── conftest.py│   └── unit│       ├── repository
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   └── test_postgres_UserRepository.py
│       ├── test_user_controller.py
│       └── test_user_service.py

First we are going to look at our unit tests, and we are going to start from the controller layer, then the service layer, and finally the repository layer.

/tests/unit/test_user_controller.py

from src.dependencies import get_user_serviceimport pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from unittest.mock import AsyncMockdef mock_user_service():
    """Create a mock UserService for dependency injection."""
    mock_service = AsyncMock()def sample_user_response_inactive():
    """Create a sample inactive UserResponse"""
    from src.schemas import UserSchemas
    return UserSchemas.UserResponse(
        email="test@example.com",    )
# Tests for get_user method
@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_get_user_success(mock_user_service, sample_user_response_inactive):
    """Test successful user retrieval."""
    # Setup mock to return a user
    mock_user_service.get_user.return_value = sample_user_response_inactive
    # Override the dependency
    app.dependency_overrides[get_user_service] = lambda: mock_user_service
    # Make the request to the endpoint
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response = client.get("/users", params={"email": "test@example.com"})
    app.dependency_overrides.clear()
    # Verify the service method was called correctly
    mock_user_service.get_user.assert_called_once_with(email=sample_user_response_inactive.email)    assert response.status_code == 200
    user_data = response.json()
    assert user_data["email"] == sample_user_response_inactive.email
    assert user_data["is_active"] == sample_user_response_inactive.is_active

In this test we are using two  to:

  1. Mock a UserService object
  2. Return a UserResponse model.

These can be used throughout our tests. I will now go through what happens, step by step, in our first unit test.

  1. Our mock UserService instance mock_user_service is going to return our UserResponse model, when mock_user_service.get_user is called.
  2. Since our controller on get_user_service , we must now override this dependency, such that it now depends on our mock_user_service .
  3. We make the request to the endpoint, and since we are now using our mock_user_service , we return the UserResponse defined in our when mock_user_service.get_user(email='test@example.com') is called.
  4. We clear the so that after the test, the controller depends on the real UserService again, not our mock.
  5. We assert that the get_user methods was called exactly once as expected.
  6. We assert that the response is as expected.

This example shows how we can test our controller, by mocking dependencies (get_user_service ), Objects (UserService ), and object methods ( UserService.get_user ).

/tests/unit/test_user_service.py

import pytestfrom unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patchdef user_service():
    """Create a UserService with a mocked repository."""
    from src.service.UserService import UserService
    from src.repository.interfaces.interface_UserRepository import UserRepository
    # Create a mock repository directly
    mock_repo = AsyncMock(spec=UserRepository)
    # Return the service with the mock repo
    return UserService(user_repository=mock_repo)def sample_user_inactive_nopw():
    """Create a sample User (result from user_repo.get_user) for testing."""
    from src.schemas import UserSchemas        email="test@example.com",    )
# Tests for get_user method
@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_get_user_success(
    user_service,
    sample_user_inactive_nopw
    ):
    """Test successful user retrieval."""
    # Setup mock to return a User
    user_service.user_repository.get_user = AsyncMock(return_value=sample_user_inactive_nopw)
    # Call the method - returns UserResponse
    user = await user_service.get_user(sample_user_inactive_nopw.email)
    # Verify the repository method was called correctly
    user_service.user_repository.get_user.assert_called_once_with(sample_user_inactive_nopw.email)    assert user.email == sample_user_inactive_nopw.email
    assert user.is_active is False

Now we are testing the service layer, and as you can see, we follow the same principles from before. The key point here is the effect of the Repository Pattern: we’re now mocking the interface, not the actual implementation. This means that we do not have to change our unit tests for our service layer, when we swap database implementations.

/tests/unit/repository/test_postgres_repository.py

import pytestfrom unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock, patch
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy.exc import IntegrityError@pytest.fixture    """Create a mock AsyncSession for testing."""
    mock = AsyncMock(spec=AsyncSession)
    # Setup the async context manager for begin()
    context_manager = AsyncMock()
    mock.begin.return_value = context_managerdef user_repo(mock_db):
    """Create a UserRepository instance with a mock db."""
    from src.repository.implementations.PostgreSQL.postgres_UserRepository import UserRepository
    return UserRepository(db=mock_db)
def db_user():
    """Create a sample UserORM object that would be returned from the database."""
    from src.repository.implementations.PostgreSQL.models.ORM_User import UserORM
    user = MagicMock(spec=UserORM)
    user.email = "test@example.com"    user.hashed_password = "hashed_password_value"
# Tests for get_user method
@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_get_user_success(user_repo, mock_db, db_user):
    """Test successful user retrieval."""
    # Import inside test function to avoid early import
    from src.schemas import UserSchemas
    # Setup mock to return a user
    mock_result = MagicMock()
    mock_result.scalar_one_or_none.return_value = db_user
    mock_db.execute.return_value = mock_result    user = await user_repo.get_user("test@example.com")    assert user.email == "test@example.com"
    assert user.is_active is True
    mock_db.execute.assert_called_once()

Here we are testing our actual PostgreSQL implementation, by mocking the PostgreSQL database. The context_manager is used for testing database , which we’ll use later on in this series. A database transaction is simply when multiple database operations are executed, and if one fails, all subsequent operations are cancelled, and all previous operations are rolled back. Also note that we are mocking the database response using UserORM to return our db_user as defined in the . This means when we call mock_db.execute we return our db_user . This in turn means that get_user translates this UserORM object into a User defined by our .

In our unit tests it should now be clear that we are benefitting from the Repository Pattern, and that due to the loose coupling measures we’ve made earlier, we can very easily write unit tests for our service layer without worrying about the actual database implementation.

For integration tests, we necessarily depend on our actual database implementations, since we’re testing the system end-to-end. We are not going to set up a test database, which is obviously best practise, since we want to make real database operations. However, its been chosen to implement our tests in a way that would easily allows us to use a test database.

For this reason we are going to override our db_context that you might recall from our previous article. The db_context was our dependency function returning either an AsyncSession or a DynamoDBClient, dependent on whether our Settings were set to use either of those as its database backend. For the purpose of this article, we are simple going to focus on our PostgreSQL implementation.

/tests/integration/conftest.py

import pytest(...)settings = get_settings()
# Define override_db_context at module level - will be set properly later
async def override_db_context():
if settings.DATABASE_TYPE == DatabaseType.POSTGRES:
    test_engine = create_async_engine(settings.POSTGRES_DATABASE_URL_FOR_TESTING, echo=True)
    TestAsyncSessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(        expire_on_commit=False,    )
    @pytest.fixture(scope="session")
    async def setup_database():
        """Set up the test database."""
        async with test_engine.begin() as conn:
            await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.drop_all)
            await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all)        # Clear tables instead of dropping them
        async with test_engine.begin() as conn:
            for table in reversed(Base.metadata.sorted_tables):
                await conn.execute(text('TRUNCATE TABLE auth.users CASCADE'))
    async def db_session(setup_database):
        """Create a fresh database session for each test."""
        connection = await test_engine.connect()
        transaction = await connection.begin()
        session = AsyncSession(bind=connection, expire_on_commit=False)
            yield session            await session.close()
            await transaction.rollback()
            await connection.close()
    # Override the module-level function
    async def postgresql_context():
        async with TestAsyncSessionLocal() as session:
            yield session
    # Set the module-level function
    override_db_context = postgresql_context
elif settings.DATABASE_TYPE == DatabaseType.DYNAMODB:
    # Set the module-level function
    override_db_context = dynamodb_context    raise ValueError(f"Invalid DATABASE_TYPE: {settings.DATABASE_TYPE}. Must be one of {[e.value for e in DatabaseType]}")@pytest.fixture
async def async_client(setup_database):
    """Create an async client for testing the API."""
    # Override the db_context dependency
    app.dependency_overrides[db_context] = override_db_context
    transport = ASGITransport(app=app)
    async with AsyncClient(transport=transport, base_url="http://test", follow_redirects=True) as client:    app.dependency_overrides.clear()

conftest.py configures our testing environment for our integration tests. So going through it step by step, if our database backend is PostgreSQL:

  1. We create an and a , just like we do in our start-up tasks. This is also where we allow to use another database, e.g. one specifically for testing.
  2. The setup_database fixture clears auth.users table prior to running tests.
  3. The db_session fixture creates a new per request.
  4. The postgresql_context acts as our new db_context; we override the original dependency with this context for testing. . That means when we the postgresql_context into our get_user_repository , and thus we use the our test database when testing our service.
  5. Finally, in async_client , we make the override and yield our client. This is AsyncClient is then the one we use to make test requests to our API, as you shall see in our actual integration tests. Finally, after each test, we remove our dependency override, so that we no longer depend on our testing context.

/tests/integration/test_api.py

import pytestfrom httpx import AsyncClient
@pytest.mark.asyncio(loop_scope="session")
async def test_get_nonexistent_user(async_client: AsyncClient):
    """Test retrieving a user that doesn't exist"""
        "email": "nonexistent@example.com"
    response = await async_client.get(
        "/users",    )
    assert response.status_code == 404
    data = response.json()    assert data["error"] == f"User with email {test_user_data['email']} not found"

Here we use the configurations made in conftest.py from before, to make actual API request, and thus test our service. In this example, we’re testing whether our service returns the expected error when a non-existent user is requested.

Now, we want to run these files in our container. In order to do so we are going to build on top of our previous Dockerfile, where we were heavily inspired by uv’s multistage.Dockerfile . For simplicity, we are going to break the Dockerfile into logical bits.

############################################ BUILDER stage
###########################################
FROM ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.12-bookworm-slim AS builder
ENV UV_COMPILE_BYTECODE=1 UV_LINK_MODE=copy
# Disable Python downloads, because we want to use the system interpreter
# across both images. If using a managed Python version, it needs to be
# copied from the build image into the final image; see `standalone.Dockerfile`
# for an example.
ENV UV_PYTHON_DOWNLOADS=0
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock \
    --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml \
    uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-dev
ADD . /app
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    uv sync --frozen --no-dev
############################################ UNIT TEST stage
###########################################
FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS unit-test
# It is important to use the image that matches the builder, as the path to the
# Python executable must be the same, e.g., using `python:3.11-slim-bookworm`
# Copy the application from the builder
COPY --from=builder --chown=app:app /app /app
# Place executables in the environment at the front of the path
ENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"
# Run unit tests by default (using config from pyproject.toml)
CMD ["python", "-m", "pytest", "app/tests/unit"]

We simply run our unit tests with the command python -m pytest app/tests/unit. Obviously we do not have to have our service running to run unit tests, as we are not making actual requests.

############################################ INTEGRATION TEST stage
###########################################
FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS integration-test
# Copy the application from the builder
COPY --from=builder --chown=app:app /app /app
# Install bash for scripting
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y bashENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"
# Create test runner script
RUN echo '#!/bin/bash
\
echo "👉 Starting server..."
\
uvicorn src.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 & 
\n\
echo "👉 Waiting for server to start..."
\
sleep 5
\echo "👉 Running integration tests..."
\
python -m pytest app/tests/integration
\
TEST_EXIT_CODE=$?
\echo "👉 Stopping server..."
\n' > /app/run_integration_tests.sh && chmod +x /app/run_integration_tests.sh
# Run integration tests by default
CMD ["/app/run_integration_tests.sh"]

To run our integration tests, we are first starting our server, and then running the tests. This is obviously necessary because our integration tests obviously need our server to be running to send requests. For this we are making a short script to be executed on container start:

  1. We run the integration tests

You can find the actual Dockerfile here, as it will also include a stage that runs both unit- and integration tests.

While this article focused more on making tests “work right” than on writing the “right” tests, it’s still important to consider which tests are most critical for your application. Ideally, we would have 100% code coverage, but oftentimes we want ship software before that happens — if it ever happens.

From our growing Project Architecture I hope you can see how important it is, to consider everything from using the right patterns to putting stuff in the right place — you can easily give yourself and others a hard time maintaining even a simple application. Also, our microservice’s core functionality has barely changed since the start of this series-which highlights the big gap between a school-project FastAPI app and an enterprise-grade FastAPI service.

In the next articles we are changing focus, as we will now focus on making our microservice work in a system of microservices. Up until now, we have made our service work as a single service, but in any microservice architecture, you’ll have multiple microservices . So in the following articles we shall discover how we can use event-driven architecture to make multiple loosely coupled microservices work together. Microservices obviously be loosely coupled, because not being defeats the whole point — then we should have a .

items.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (8/10): SAGA- and Outbox Pattern
items.author: Asbjorn Bering
items.content:
Building an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), Terraform

Before we make the actual implementation, we are going to discuss how we can achieve loose coupling between microservices using event-driven architecture. First we are going to introduce some relevant topics to make a general understanding.

Central Concepts in Event-Driven Architecture

A is central to a pub/sub system: it receives incoming published by and routes them to the appropriate , which can subscribe to.

An typically consists of a and a . (Events can have additional metadata, but for simplicity, we’ll focus on these two parts.) The topic defines which the event goes into, i.e. a consumer will to specific topics and the is the data related to the event.

For example, when we create a subscription, we might publish an event with the topic created_subscription_success and the new subscription data (such as subscription_id and email) as the payload. Now other services these created_subscription_success events, will now be able to react to the creation of a subscription. For example we our User microservice consumes this event, and creates a new user.

In an event-driven system, a (or ) is an entity that sends events to the broker. A to a and processes incoming events.

A is defined by a and a . When a broker receives an event, it places the event into all queues matching the event’s topic. This means we can have multiple queues representing the same topic, but with different group IDs. The purpose of the group ID is then to ensure:

  1. multiple instances of the same service are running, only one instance processes each event. This prevents duplicate processing and potential conflicts. The group ID ensures that all instances of the same service, subscribe to the same queue, making it impossible to pull the same event twice.
  2. If multiple different services subscribe to the same topic, each service receives its own copy of the event. The group ID allows each service to have its own queue for the topic, and the broker ensures the event is delivered to all relevant queues.

In event-driven systems we face some common challenges: The dual write problem, and distributed transactions.

The core idea of event-driven architecture is that a microservice performs an action and then informs other microservices about it, so they can react accordingly. A simple example is when our User Service create a user, our Subscription Service must create a subscription. The problem arises if our Subscription Service fails to create the subscription: we end up with an inconsistent state, where a user exists but their subscription does not. This is where the SAGA Choreography pattern becomes relevant: it enables us to roll back previous operations across microservices, helping to solve the distributed transaction problem.

The dual write problem occurs when we want to update two external systems atomically. For example, we might want to write to the database and publish an event to the broker as a single, reliable operation. An example could be we want to create a subscription, and publish a created_subscription_success event. The problem occurs when one of the operations fail, i.e. we successfully create the subscription, but we are unable to publish created_subscription_success event — this could simply be because the broker is down. This is where the Outbox Table pattern becomes relevant, as it ensures this behaviour does not occur.

SAGA Choreography Pattern

Put simply, in the SAGA Choreography pattern each local transaction publishes domain events, which then trigger related transactions in other services. This means we can achieve rollbacks by publishing events that trigger compensating actions in other services. For example, if our User Service publishes a user_created_from_new_subscription_failed event, our Subscription Service can consume this event and delete the related subscription, effectively rolling back the previous insertion. This way we ensure database consistency across multiple services.

To address the dual write problem, the Outbox Table pattern introduces a dedicated outbox table in the database to store events. This way we can make a database operation and write to the outbox table in atomic manner. We then use (Change Data Capture) to read from the outbox table and ensure each event is published to the broker. This way we ensure that each database operation has a related event in the outbox table. If CDC fails to publish an event, we can implement retry logic or compensating actions to maintain consistency. Therefore we ensure consistency across our two systems by implementing the outbox pattern.

In this article, we explored how to achieve loose coupling between microservices using event-driven architecture. We covered common challenges and how patterns like SAGA Choreography and Outbox Table can address them. In the next article, we’ll dive into the actual implementation with Kafka and Debezium in our FastAPI microservice architecture.

items.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (9/10): Debezium & Outbox Table…
items.author: Asbjorn Bering
items.content:
Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (9/10): Debezium & Outbox Table Pattern

Building an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), Terraform

In the previous article we discussed event-driven architecture to make our microservices loosely coupled. We also discussed common challenges and patterns to address these. In this article we are going to implement the SAGA Choreography pattern and the Outbox Table pattern using as our broker and for CDC. This is a common enterprise-grade stack when dealing with PostgreSQL database backends.

Debezium is not compatible with DynamoDB, so for CDC with DynamoDB, we would use DynamoDB Streams in combination with AWS Lambda, for event distribution we could then use Kafka or SNS and SQS. In this article, we focus on the Kafka and Debezium stack with PostgreSQL.

Outbox Table Definition & OutboxORM

Firstly we are going to define our table, and the relating ORM.

-- Create the schema if not existsCREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS auth;
-- Create the users table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS auth.users (
    email VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY,
    hashed_password VARCHAR(200), -- hashed (including salt));
-- create the users outbox table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS auth.users_outbox (    aggregatetype TEXT NOT NULL,
    aggregateid TEXT NOT NULL,
    eventtype TEXT NOT NULL,    created_at BIGINT NOT NULL

The outbox table consist of the following:

  1.  — unique id for each record
  2.  — used as topic suffix
  3.  — user id, in our case the user’s email
  4.  — type of event, events with the same topic might have different event types. This way consumers can subscribe to one topic, and handle events differently based on the event type.
  5.  — the data related to the event as JSON.
  6.  — UNIX timestamp in milliseconds

This table definition is highly dependent on our choice of tool for CDC. In our case this table definition is heavily inspired by the official Debezium documentation.

/src/repository/implementations/PostgreSQL/models/ORM_User.py

import uuid6from datetime import datetime ,timezone
from sqlalchemy import Column, String, Boolean, BigInteger, JSON
from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import UUID
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
Base = declarative_base()
    __tablename__ = "users"
    __table_args__ = {"schema": "auth"}
    email = Column(String, primary_key=True, unique=True, index=True, nullable=False)
    hashed_password = Column(String, nullable=True)
    is_active = Column(Boolean, default=True)
class UsersOutboxORM(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users_outbox"
    __table_args__ = {"schema": "auth"}  # Specifies the schema
    id = Column(UUID(as_uuid=True), primary_key=True, default=uuid6.uuid6)
    aggregatetype = Column(String, nullable=False)     # e.g., "user" -> TOPIC
    aggregateid = Column(String, nullable=False)       # e.g., user_id
    eventtype = Column(String, nullable=False)              # e.g., "user_created_success"
    payload = Column(JSON, nullable=False)             # Event data as JSON
    created_at = Column(BigInteger, nullable=False, default=lambda: int(datetime.now(timezone.utc).timestamp() * 1000))

Similar to how we created our UserORM , we must also define a UserOutboxORM to interact with our Outbox table in a pythonic manner. Note that we are using default values for id and created_at , as these should just be set automatically when we create a record.

/src/schemas/UserSchemas.py:

(...)    email: str
    hashed_password: Optional[str] = None
    is_active: Optional[bool] = None
class UserResponse(BaseModel):
    email: str
    is_active: Optional[bool] = None
    aggregatetype: str    eventtype_prefix: str

We also create our Outbox model, as we are now not only creating a User , but also an Outbox record when we insert a User in our database.

/src/service/UserService.py

(...)
    def __init__(self, user_repository: interface_UserRepository):
        self.user_repository = user_repository            self,
            User_instance: UserSchemas.User,
            eventtype_prefix: str,
            payload_add: Dict[str, Any] = None
        ) -> None:            await self.user_repository.create_user(
                User_instance = User_instance,
                Outbox_instance = UserSchemas.Outbox(
                    aggregatetype = "user",
                    aggregateid = User_instance.email,
                    eventtype_prefix = eventtype_prefix,                        "email": User_instance.email,
                        "is_active": True if User_instance.is_active else False,
                        **(payload_add or {})                )
        except ResourceAlreadyExistsException:
            raise            logger.exception(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}")
            raise BaseAppException(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}") from e

In our create_user function we are now calling user.repository.create_user with not only the User instance as argument, but also an Outbox instance. This effectively tells our database backend, which records to put in the database.

/src/repository/implementations/PostgreSQL/postgres_UserRepository.py

(...)
class UserRepository(interface_UserRepository.UserRepository):
    def __init__(self, db: AsyncSession):
        self.db = db            self,
            User_instance: UserSchemas.User,
            Outbox_instance: UserSchemas.Outbox            db_user = UserORM(
                email=User_instance.email,
                hashed_password=User_instance.hashed_password,
                is_active=True if User_instance.is_active else False
            )
            outbox_event = UsersOutboxORM(
                aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype,
                aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid,
                eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_success",
                payload = Outbox_instance.payload
            )            async with self.db.begin():  # This ensures atomicity                self.db.add(outbox_event)
        except IntegrityError as e:
            if "UniqueViolationError" in str(e.orig):
                logger.warning(f"User with email {User_instance.email} already exists")
                fail_event = UsersOutboxORM(
                    aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype,
                    aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid,
                    eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_failed",
                    payload = Outbox_instance.payload.update({
                        "exception": "ResourceAlreadyExistsException"                )
                async with self.db.begin():
                    self.db.add(fail_event)
                raise ResourceAlreadyExistsException(f"User with email {User_instance.email} already exists")
            else:
                # Some other kind of IntegrityError (e.g., null value, foreign key constraint, etc)
                logger.exception(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}")
                fail_event = UsersOutboxORM(
                    aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype,
                    aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid,
                    eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_failed",
                    payload = Outbox_instance.payload.update({
                        "exception": "BaseAppException"                )
                async with self.db.begin():
                    self.db.add(fail_event)
                raise BaseAppException(f"Database integrity error: {str(e)}") from e
        except ResourceAlreadyExistsException:
            raise            logger.exception(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}")
            fail_event = UsersOutboxORM(
                aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype,
                aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid,
                eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_failed",
                payload = Outbox_instance.payload.update({
                    "exception": "BaseAppException"            )
            async with self.db.begin():
                self.db.add(fail_event)
            raise BaseAppException(f"Internal database error: {str(e)}") from e

This is where we actually write to our auth.users and auth.users_outbox in a transactional manner. We also write to auth.users_outbox table in the case where we fail to write to these tables. When we fail to write to our database, we append the exception to the payload. This means consumers of this event are able to handle these failures based on not only the event type, but also the exception when a fail event occurs. For example if we try to create a User that already exists, when our User Service consumes a subcription_created_success event, we do not want our Subscription Service to perform a roll back and delete the subscription. But if something else goes wrong, e.g. a database connection error, we would want a roll back action.

We only need to care about writing to the Outbox table, because Debezium will take care of capturing the insertion and publish an event to Kafka.

Now we have set up auth.users_outboxtable. In the next section we are going to configure Debezium, so we are publishing messages to Kafka, when records are inserted in the auth.users_outbox table.

To make Debezium work, we must grant a database user the correct privileges, following the principle of least privilege.

-- Start transaction for atomicityBEGIN;(...)
CREATE ROLE debezium_user WITH REPLICATION LOGIN PASSWORD 'secure_debezium_password';
-- Allow connection to the database
(...)
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE crypto_db TO debezium_user;
-- Allow usage of the 'auth' schema
(...)
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA auth TO debezium_user;
-- Pre-create publication for Debezium (execute as superuser)
CREATE PUBLICATION dbz_publication_user FOR TABLE auth.users_outbox;
CREATE PUBLICATION dbz_publication_subscription FOR TABLE auth.subscriptions_outbox;
-- Grant debezium_user minimal privileges needed for CDC
GRANT SELECT ON TABLE auth.users_outbox TO debezium_user;
GRANT SELECT ON TABLE auth.subscriptions_outbox TO debezium_user;
COMMIT;
