items.title: Securing a WebSocket Chat with JWT and Lambda Authorizer on API Gateway
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
This tutorial is the third installment in a series designed to help developers build secure and scalable applications using AWS serverless technologies. If you’re joining this tutorial directly, we highly recommend reviewing the first two parts to fully understand the foundations upon which this project is built:

In this third tutorial, we will combine concepts from both previous guides. Specifically, we will take the real-time WebSocket-based chat application built in Tutorial 1 and enhance it with the security mechanisms developed in Tutorial 2. The result will be a chat system where users must authenticate via JWT before establishing a WebSocket connection — ensuring that only authorized users can connect and interact with the chat backend.

Choosing the Right Authentication Strategy

AWS offers two principal methods for securing WebSocket APIs via API Gateway:

For this project, we will implement Method 2: Custom Lambda Authorizers. This approach is well-suited for systems that already use  — which is common in modern web and mobile applications. It allows developers to apply their existing authentication flows with minimal disruption, while also offering:

  • , avoiding session storage complexities.
  • , as no shared session management is required across Lambda invocations.
  • , since the token verification logic is completely customizable in the Lambda Authorizer.
  • , making it easy to integrate with any identity provider or pre-existing JWT issuance pipeline.

While IAM-based authorization offers tight integration with AWS services and centralized policy management, its requirement for Signature Version 4 (SigV4) in the client and tighter AWS account coupling makes it less approachable for developers managing cross-platform clients or systems not originally built on the AWS security model.

With this foundation in mind, let’s now move forward and integrate secure JWT-based access control into our WebSocket chat architecture. In the next section, we’ll begin the implementation by setting up the authentication Lambda functions and registering a custom authorizer for the $connect route of our WebSocket API.

Key differences from Lambda authorizer of previous tutorial

Lambda authorizer function in WebSocket APIs is similar to that for REST APIs, with the following exceptions:

  • You can only use a Lambda authorizer function for the $connect route.
  • You cannot use path variables (event.pathParameters), because the path is fixed.
  • event.methodArn is different from its REST API equivalent, because it has no HTTP method. In the case of connect,methodArnendswith”,”connect”: arn:aws:execute-api:region:account-id:api-id/stage-name/$connect
  • The context variables in event.requestContext are different from those for REST APIs.

Step-by-step Tutorial: Securing a WebSocket Chat with JWT and Lambda Authorizer

Make sure you’ve completed the following tutorials:

At this point, you should have:

  • A WebSocket API Gateway: WebSocket API — responsible for handling real-time chat connections and messaging using WebSocket protocol.
  • A REST API Gateway: Token Issuer API — used to authenticate users and issue JWTs required to authorize WebSocket connections.

Step 1: Create the Lambda Authorizer

Create a new Lambda function to serve as your custom authorizer. Use the same approach from Tutorial 2, but tailored for WebSocket APIs:

  • jwt_auth_token_websocket
  • Python 3.13 or superior
  • Attach the AWS managed policy AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
  • JWT_SECRET: Use the same secret used to sign JWTs in your system.

Step 2: Create the Lambda Authorizer in API Gateway

  1. Navigate to your WebSocket API in API Gateway Console.
  2. Go to the section and click .
  3. Configure the authorizer with the following:
  • Select jwt_auth_token_websocket
  •  Header

Step 3: Assign Authorizer to $connect Route

  1. In the API Gateway console, navigate to the  section.
  2. Select the $connect route.
  3. In the panel, click next to .
  4. Select protect_lambda from the dropdown.

Step 4: Deploy the WebSocket API

  1. Go to the section of your WebSocket API.
  2. Choose your deployment stage (e.g., dev).
  3. Click to apply the changes.

Step 5: Test the Secure WebSocket Connection On Terminal

You can use wscat to test the connection to the WebSocket API with and without valid tokens.

✅ Valid Token (Should connect successfully)

  1. Use your /auth endpoint (from API Gateway REST - created in Tutorial 2) to obtain a valid JWT.
  2. Connect to API Gateway WebSocket (created in Tutorial 1) using wscat with the Authorization header:
wscat -H "Authorization: Bearer <your_valid_jwt>" -c wss://<your-api-id>.execute-api.<region>.amazonaws.com/dev/

❌ Invalid or No Token (Should be rejected)

wscat -H "Authorization: Bearer invalidtoken" -c wss://<your-api-id>.execute-api.<region>.amazonaws.com/dev/
wscat -c wss://<your-api-id>.execute-api.<region>.amazonaws.com/dev/

In both cases, the request will fail, confirming that your Lambda Authorizer is correctly enforcing token validation.

Step 6: Enable CORS for the Login Endpoint

Although the authentication flow is functioning correctly in tools like Postman (as demonstrated in Tutorial 2), it is essential to enable CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) to support web-based clients such as browsers.

Step 6.1: Access and Select the Login Resource

Go to the AWS Management Console and open the API Gateway service. Locate the REST API that handles your login logic. In the left-hand menu, click on , then select the resource associated with the login endpoint, for example, /auth.

Step 6.2: Enable CORS on the Resource

With the /auth resource selected, click on the dropdown menu and choose . This will open the CORS configuration dialog.

Step 6.3: Configure CORS Settings

  • Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to * to allow requests from all origins. This is suitable for development purposes. For production, specify your trusted domain (e.g., https://yourdomain.com).
  • Select the method, as it is the one used for submitting authentication requests.
  • Leave the default settings for headers such as Access-Control-Allow-Headers and Access-Control-Allow-Methods enabled.

Step 6.4: Apply and Deploy Changes

Click on Enable CORS and replace existing CORS headers to apply the configuration.

After that, navigate to the section of your REST API, select the current stage (e.g., dev), and click to publish the changes.

: Enabling CORS will automatically create an OPTIONS method for your resource, which is required by browsers to perform preflight requests. This ensures that your front-end application can make authenticated cross-origin requests to the /auth endpoint without encountering CORS errors.

Step 7: Implement a Simple Chat App with HTML, JS, and CSS

In this step, we integrate a simple front-end chat application to interact with our WebSocket API. Since the focus of this tutorial is on setting up the WebSocket API in AWS, I won’t go into detailed explanations of the front-end code. However, you can find the full implementation in the GitHub repository and test the application directly via GitHub Pages at this link.

Important Note on Using WebSockets in Browsers

One important consideration when implementing WebSocket clients using pure HTML and JavaScript in the browser is that browsers do not support setting custom headers during the WebSocket handshake. This includes the Authorization header, which is commonly used to send JWTs. This limitation is defined by the WebSocket API standard and is enforced for security and standardization reasons. For more information, refer to the Mozilla Developer Network (MDN) documentation on WebSocket.

This limitation applies specifically to WebSocket connections in the browser. For REST APIs, using the Authorization header from the browser is fully supported and considered a best practice.

Because of this restriction, we cannot send the JWT token via the Authorization header when establishing a WebSocket connection from a browser. The recommended workaround is to pass the token as a , such as:

wss://your-api-id.execute-api.region.amazonaws.com/dev/?token=your_jwt

However, this is not ideal from a security perspective, as query strings are more likely to be exposed in browser history, logs, or analytics tools. Therefore, for production-grade applications, we recommend using a backend technology that allows custom headers, such as:

  • Python (websocket-client)
  • Node.js (ws or socket.io-client)
  • Java (Tyrus or Jetty WebSocketClient)

These environments provide full control over the WebSocket handshake and allow sending JWTs securely via the Authorization header.

Step 7.1: Update the Lambda Authorizer to Support Query String

If you decide to support browser-based clients by passing the JWT token as a query string, you need to update the Lambda Authorizer code to extract the token from the query string.

In your Python Lambda Authorizer, update the logic that retrieves the token as follows:

# Add this line to handle token via query string:query_params = event.get('queryStringParameters') or {}
# Update token extraction logic:
auth_header = headers.get('Authorization') or headers.get('authorization') or query_params.get('token')

This ensures that the authorizer can accept the JWT from a query string parameter named token.

Step 7.2: Update the API Gateway Authorizer Configuration

To allow API Gateway to extract the token from the query string, update the Lambda Authorizer’s :

  1. Go to the .
  2. Navigate to your .
  3. Select the tab.
  4. Edit the identity source and replace:
route.request.header.Authorization
route.request.querystring.token
⚠️ Note: You must remove the header.Authorization source entirely. If multiple identity sources are defined, all must be present in the request for the authorizer to be invoked.

Step 7.3: Deploy the API Gateway Changes

After updating the authorizer and its identity source, you must deploy the changes:

  1. Go to the section of your WebSocket API.
  2. Select the current stage (e.g., dev).
  3. Click to publish your changes.

Step 7.4: Test the Query String Token via Terminal

Before testing the browser-based implementation, it’s useful to verify the new query string token approach directly from the terminal. This ensures that your Lambda Authorizer is correctly configured to accept tokens from the query string.

You can use the following command with wscat to initiate a WebSocket connection:

wscat -c "wss://zb9brhvdsi.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/dev/?Authorization=Bearer%20eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoidGVzdHVzZXIiLCJleHAiOjE3NDQ0MDk4Mzd9.xcSxvKNIaTqo4DT-zNCESn3R5i1r8b0bLiWbe2xNUy0"

Replace the JWT in the URL with a valid token issued by your authentication system.

If the token is valid and the API Gateway configuration is correct, you should see the WebSocket connection open successfully. If the connection fails, verify the token structure and Lambda Authorizer logs in CloudWatch.

Step 7.5: Test with the HTML Front-End

Once the back-end changes are complete, you can test the browser-based application. The application consists of two main pages:

  • : Responsible for the login flow and acquiring the JWT token from the authentication API.
  • : Responsible for establishing the WebSocket connection and interacting with the chat backend using the token stored in local storage.

This setup allows a user to log in, obtain a token, and connect securely to the WebSocket API. You can review the complete implementation or run the live application using the following resources:

For reference, when connecting to the WebSocket server, your JavaScript code should now include the JWT token as a query string parameter:

const token = localStorage.getItem("authToken");const encodedToken = encodeURIComponent(`Bearer ${token}`);
const wsUrl = `wss://your-api-id.execute-api.region.amazonaws.com/dev/?token=${encodedToken}`;
const socket = new WebSocket(wsUrl);

This allows the browser-based client to authenticate securely using the Lambda Authorizer while staying within the constraints of the WebSocket API in the browser.

If you now connect using this method and the token is valid, the $connect request will succeed and establish a secure WebSocket session.

In this tutorial, you secured a WebSocket-based chat application using JWT authentication and a custom Lambda Authorizer on the $connect route of API Gateway. This setup ensures that only authenticated users can establish connections, aligning with modern security best practices for real-time serverless applications.

With a stateless and scalable architecture, this solution is flexible enough to integrate with any identity provider or token issuance flow. As you continue building, consider implementing fine-grained access controls, token scopes, and centralized logging to further strengthen your application’s security posture.

If you found this guide helpful, stay connected for more insights on AI, cloud security, and AWS automation:

items.title: How to Run TimescaleDB on a Kubernetes Cluster with Automated Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
items.author: Maksym Perehinets
items.content:
I faced this issue while migrating from a VM to Kubernetes (K8s). Running databases in a stateless K8s environment is a real challenge. In this article, I will focus on the problems I encountered after the stateful storage was figured out. If you are interested in how I solved the problem with high-performance persistent storage with minimal latency in K8s, leave a comment, and I will write about it.

The database must be in the K8s cloud, but the available options are too expensive, and network latency is too high. Despite that, we want to retain as much control over our database as possible.

Choosing CloudNativePG (CNPG) was not an immediate solution; it was rather hard to choose from the other options out there. I will not include a comparison table, as I believe this has already been done quite a few times by all of those open-source software (OSS) tools. Why did I choose it? It’s relatively new compared to other tools, yet it’s a Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) sandbox project, and it also has quite a large community behind it. In addition, feedback about it is positive, which is something that most of its competitors do not have.

Now that we have an initial understanding of why we chose CNPG, we can take a look at its setup in K8s. For this example, I will use Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with managed-csi for simplicity.

To begin with, let’s take a look at a default CNPG cluster deployment.

First, we need to create an image catalog that will allow us to use the TimescaleDB image, as its default tag is not compliant with CNPG requirements. It needs a tag with the major.minor PostgreSQL version, but it also allows a patch version. So, the tag should be similar to `12.3.2.1–1` or `12.3`. However, the default tag for TimescaleDB is `pg15-ts2.18-all` or `pg15-ts2.18`. To fix this, we will use the `ImageCatalog` Custom Resource Definition (CRD) that the CNPG operator installs.

apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1kind: ImageCatalog  name: cloudnative-pg-timescaledb-pg15  images:
    - major: 15 # postgres major version
      image: timescale/timescaledb-ha:pg15-ts2.18-all

After this is done, we can start setting up our TimescaleDB cluster CRD. In this example, we will create a user with a password as well as a Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) archive backed by an Azure storage account for point-in-time recovery.

First, we will need a secret for our user database. Let’s call it `dev` for now. (Also, please change this secret if you plan to run it exposed via Ingress or a NodePort; otherwise, it will likely be stolen in a few minutes):

apiVersion: v1kind: Secret  name: app-user-secret
type: kubernetes.io/basic-auth
data:  username: bXl1c2Vy  password: U3Ryb25nUGFzc3dvcmQxMjMh

Now that we have our secrets created, the next step will be a secret with an SAS key for our blob storage.

Note: The SAS token requires permissions to read, add, create, write, delete, and list.
apiVersion: v1kind: Secret  name: blob-sasdata:

Also, let’s add a simple table and some data to it during the Bootstrap stage. For this, we will need ConfigMap resources:

apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap  name: init-schema  init-schema.sql: |
    CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS timescaledb;
    CREATE TABLE device_metrics (
        time        TIMESTAMPTZ       NOT NULL,
        device_id   TEXT              NOT NULL,
        temperature DOUBLE PRECISION  NULL,
        humidity    DOUBLE PRECISION  NULL
    );
    SELECT create_hypertable('device_metrics', by_range('time'));
    INSERT INTO device_metrics (time, device_id, temperature, humidity) VALUES
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '10 minutes', 'device_A', 25.5, 60.1),
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '10 minutes', 'device_B', 22.3, 55.9),
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '8 minutes',  'device_A', 25.7, 60.5),
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '6 minutes',  'device_B', 22.4, 56.0),
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '4 minutes',  'device_A', 25.9, 61.0),
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '4 minutes',  'device_C', 19.5, 70.2),
        (NOW() - INTERVAL '2 minutes',  'device_B', 22.6, 56.3),
        (NOW(),                        'device_A', 26.0, 61.2),
        (NOW(),                        'device_C', 19.8, 70.0);

Now that we have those, we can set up our cluster resources themselves:

apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1kind: Cluster  name: timescaledb-cluster-pg15  instances: 1    apiGroup: postgresql.cnpg.io    name: cloudnative-pg-timescaledb-pg15  postgresUID: 1000  resources:      cpu: "1"    limits:      memory: 7Gi    shared_preload_libraries:    parameters:      max_wal_senders: "3"       - host all all all scram-sha-256    barmanObjectStore:
      destinationPath: "https://<your storage account name>.blob.core.windows.net/timescaleshowcase"
      azureCredentials:          name: blob-sas    initdb:      owner: myuser # This should match the username in app-user-secret        name: app-user-secret 
      postInitApplicationSQLRefs:
        configMapRefs:          key: init-schema.sql    size: 30Gi
    storageClass: managed-csi

We are almost there. The last step before the showcase will be creating a scheduled backup for our database:

apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1kind: ScheduledBackup  name: timescaledb-daily-backup  schedule: "0 0 3 * * *"
  backupOwnerReference: cluster
  cluster:
    name: timescaledb-cluster-pg15

Now, let’s install CNPG into the cluster.

helm repo add cnpg https://cloudnative-pg.github.io/chartshelm upgrade - install cnpg \
 - namespace cnpg-system \
 - create-namespace \

Now we can apply our configuration and take a look at what we’ve got.

So we have it deployed, we can see our db deployed and running:

As well as services, so it can be accessed within the cluster:

To check it out, let's create a tunnel to access it from within our local computer:

kubectl port-forward svc/timescaledb-cluster-pg15-rw 5433:5432

After that, we will be available to connect to it on localhost port 5433 and see that our db exists and contains the table we described in the config map

So let's take a look at our records:

And the last thing as we can see in our storage account we have all of the backups and WAL’s stored

As we can see it CNPG provides a simple yet robust way to manage our databases.

items.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (3/10): Project Setup
items.author: Asbjorn Bering
items.content:
Building an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), Terraform

In this article we are going to initialise our FastAPI User Service microservice using uv. Recently uv has gained massive traction, and I won’t be spending time arguing why this is the best choice of package manager in 2025 — because an abundance of others have already done exactly that.

Afterwards, I am going to build our service following the Repository Pattern. The Repository Pattern is so popular by now that people are actually starting to hate it a little bit, but fact is that it works, it’s intuitive and there is a good chance your current and/or future co-developers will know it. Therefore it is still very relevant in 2025.

Finally, I am going to run our microservice in a container using Docker. Docker is still very much part of the landscape when it comes to containerisation in 2025, and it’s just as popular and relevant as always.

Initializing our FastAPI application

First off, I am going to create a new project using uv in my /UserService folder using:

This will create the following tree:

.├── .python-version├── main.py

The .python-version file holds information about, you guessed it, your python version, while your pyproject.toml file states your application dependencies. Initially, this is all you need. But as the application grows, some more dependencies will be added.

[project]name = "userservice"description = "Add your description here"requires-python = ">=3.12"    "fastapi>=0.115.12",    "pydantic>=2.11.3"

to create a .venv folder (a virtual environment) and a uv.lock file completing the initialisation of our User Service using uv. So why is uv so amazing? to summarise the abundance of articles it comes down to simplicity and efficiency. Written in Rust, uv is 10–100x faster than pip. And as I just demonstrated, it is incredibly easy to create virtual environments and handling dependencies. Many have already written articles praising uv, so I won’t bother you too much with it — just know it is amazing and if curious to why, take a look at their official docs and do a quick search here on Medium.

Project Architecture: A Production-Ready Structure

Oftentimes I read articles about structure, but too rarely they actually present actual file and folder structure. I believe consideration when it comes to file and folder structure is crucial, and also incredibly easy to present. So this is how our User Service is going to look initially:

.├── Dockerfile.dev├── docker-compose.yaml├── src│   ├── main.py│   │   ├── implementations
│   │   │   ├── AWS_DynamoDB
│   │   │   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   │   │   └── awsdynamodb_UserRepository.py
│   │   │   ├── AWS_RDS
│   │   │   └── PostgreSQL
│   │   │       ├── __init__.py
│   │   │       └── postgres_UserRepository.py
│   │   └── interfaces
│   │       ├── __init__.py
│   │       └── interface_UserRepository.py│   │   ├── UserController.py│   ├── schemas
│   │   ├── UserSchemas.py
│   │   └── __init__.py│       ├── UserService.py│       └── utils.py

Please use this as reference when we dive into the code in the following sections. I won’t describe the reasoning behind this structure, which will hopefully be self-explanatory once we start implementing in the next sections.

/src/schemas/UserSchemas.py

from pydantic import BaseModel, model_validatorfrom typing_extensions import Self
from typing import Optional
class ResetPassword(BaseModel):
    password: str
    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def check_passwords_match(self) -> Self:
        if self.password != self.password_repeat:
            raise ValueError('Passwords do not match')    email: str
    hashed_password: Optional[str] = None
    is_active: Optional[bool] = None
class UserResponse(BaseModel):
    email: str

First of all, these are the Pydantic schemas I am going to use. Pydantic is a popular data validation library, and it is widely used for FastAPI applications. It works very well in FastAPI applications, because request bodies are automatically validated when using Pydantic, e.g. are the required fields present with the correct data types in the request body. In the above you can also see a custom model_validator which checks whether the two passwords, password and password_repeat, are equal. This is relevant when a user sends a request to change their password, as they will only be able to do so, if the two passwords they provide are equal. You will see extensive use of these throughout the application.

/src/repository/interfaces/interface_UserRepository.py

from abc import ABC, abstractmethodfrom ...schemas import UserSchemas
class UserRepository(ABC):
    async def get_user(self, email: str) -> UserSchemas.User:    async def create_user(self, User_instance: UserSchemas.User):    async def update_user(self, User_instance: UserSchemas.User):

To achieve loose coupling between the service layer and the actual database implementation, I create an interface. This means my actual implementation this interface, and it also means my service layer does not depend on the actual implementation, but on the interface. You can also see here that we have defined that the get_usermethod must return a User which we defined in our UserSchemas.pyabove.

/src/repository/implementations/PostgreSQL/postgres_UserRepository.py

from src.repository.interfaces import interface_UserRepositoryfrom src.schemas import UserSchemas
class UserRepository(interface_UserRepository.UserRepository):
    def __init__(self, db = None):
        self.db = db        self,    ) -> UserSchemas.User:             email=email,        )        self,
        User_instance: UserSchemas.User
    ) -> UserSchemas.User:            email=User_instance.email,
            is_active=True if User_instance.is_active else False
        )        self,
        User_instance: UserSchemas.User
    ) -> UserSchemas.User:            email=User_instance.email,
            is_active=User_instance.is_active
        )

So here is our actual UserRepository implementation, which implements the aforementioned interface. Initially we will not bother with making an actual PostgreSQL implementation, but simply prepare for it. The actual PostgreSQL implementation will be presented later in this series.

/src/service/UserService.py

from src.repository.interfaces import interface_UserRepositoryfrom src.schemas import UserSchemas
from .utils import saltAndHashedPW
        self,
        user_repository: interface_UserRepository
    ):
        self.user_repository = user_repository
        self,    ) -> UserSchemas.UserResponse:
        user = await self.user_repository.get_user(email)
        return UserSchemas.UserResponse(            is_active=user.is_active        self,    ) -> UserSchemas.UserResponse:
        user = await self.user_repository.create_user(
            User_instance = UserSchemas.User(            )        return UserSchemas.UserResponse(            is_active=user.is_active
    async def reset_password(
        self,        reset_password: UserSchemas.ResetPassword
    ) -> UserSchemas.UserResponse:
        hashed_pw = await saltAndHashedPW(reset_password.password)
        user = await self.user_repository.update_user(                email=email,
                hashed_password=hashed_pw,
                is_active=True        )
        return UserSchemas.UserResponse(
            email=user.email,        )
    async def deactivate_user(
        self,    ) -> UserSchemas.UserResponse:
        user = await self.user_repository.update_user(
            UserSchemas.User(                is_active=False        )
        return UserSchemas.UserResponse(
            email=user.email,        )

The Repository Pattern in action. Here you can see how my UserService depends on the interface, which is an abstraction, instead of the actual implementation. This means I can later make another implementation, e.g. DynamoDB, which the interface, and I would not need to change my service layer one bit. This is one of the major reasons it is incredibly popular, and later you shall see even more benefits when we start writing unit tests.

/src/routes/UserController.py

from fastapi import APIRouter, Dependsfrom src.schemas import UserSchemas
from src.dependencies import get_user_service
from src.service.UserService import UserService    prefix="/users"
@router.get("", status_code=200)
async def get_user(    user_service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)
    return await user_service.get_user(email=email)
@router.put("/reset-password", status_code=201)
async def reset_password(    reset_password: UserSchemas.ResetPassword,
    user_service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)
):
    return await user_service.reset_password(
        email=email,
        reset_password=reset_password
    )
@router.put("/deactivate-user", status_code=201)
async def deactivate_user(    user_service: UserService = Depends(get_user_service)
    return await user_service.deactivate_user(
        email=email

Now, finally we are at our Controller layer, and as you can see we are using , which is extremely popular in FastAPI. You see our endpoints on get_user_serviceand assigns whatever this function returns (Spoiler, it’s a UserService instance) to the user_servicevariable.

from fastapi import Dependsfrom src.repository.interfaces.interface_UserRepository import UserRepository as UserRepositoryInterface
from src.service.UserService import UserService
from src.repository.implementations.PostgreSQL.postgres_UserRepository import UserRepository as PostgresUserRepository
async def get_user_repository() -> UserRepositoryInterface:
    return PostgresUserRepository()
async def get_user_service(user_repository: UserRepositoryInterface = Depends(get_user_repository)) -> UserService:
    return UserService(user_repository)

Now it becomes clear what we are into our endpoints. Right now we are not injecting real database sessions, but later in this series you will see how we extend this to actual database implementations with real PostgreSQL and DynamoDB async sessions. It also becomes clear that we achieve loose coupling wrt. our database when we inject an abstract repository interface (rather than a concrete implementation) into our service and controller layers. This means our business logic depends only on the interface, making it easy to swap out or extend database backends-such as replacing a PostgreSQL implementation with a DynamoDB implementation without changing the rest of our application code. For now, we are injecting our PostgresUserRepository, which obviously defeats the point. Later, when we actually implement our PostgreSQL and DynamoDB database backends, we will implement a similar dependency for get_user_repository as get_user_service and then actually achieve loose coupling.

from fastapi import FastAPIfrom fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from .routes import UserController
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Startup code (runs before application startup)
    logger.info("Running startup tasks...")
    logger.info("Startup tasks completed")
    yield
    # Shutdown code (runs after application shutdown)
    logger.info("Running shutdown tasks...")
    logger.info("Shutdown tasks completed")
    # This is where you put code that was previously in @app.on_event("shutdown")
app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
    "http://localhost:8000",    CORSMiddleware,    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["GET", "PUT"],
    allow_headers=["Authorization", "Content-Type"]
app.include_router(UserController.router)

We include our UserController in our FastAPI app to make the endpoints accessible. Additionally, I make some simple CORS middleware, which allows us to access our endpoints from , which means I will later be able to interact with my container from outside the container. This is necessary if I want to test my endpoints from my local machine using Postman. Finally, I set up lifespan to prepare for start up and shutdown tasks.

How is a request handled?

In the previous section I presented my initial codebase for my FastAPI User Service. I will now go through, step by step, when my endpoint receives a request. I will use GET /users?email=test@example.comas my example:

  1. End-user makes a GET /users?email=test@example.com request.
  2. UserController sets user_service as a new UserService instance by calling the get_user_service function which is injected into the endpoint. In this example the user_service.repository is the PostgreSQL implementation of the interface_UserRepository.
  3. user_service.get_user(email=’test@example.com’) is awaited.
  4. The user_service.get_usermethod awaits user_service.repository.get_user(email=’test@example.com’) and since our repository is the PostgreSQL implementation, that is the specific method that is called. This method in the PostgreSQL implementation simply returns a Useras defined in UserSchemas.py with email set as the one used in the request, in this case
  5. user_service_get_userthen transforms the Userinto a UserResponse, which is then returned to the UserController.
  6. The UserController returns the UserResponseto the end-user.

Containerization Strategy

In the previous section I built my initial simple User Service, and in this section we will run our application in a container using Docker and start interacting with it. This is our Dockerfile, heavily inspired by uv’s multistage Dockerfile:

############################################ BUILDER stage
###########################################
FROM ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.12-bookworm-slim AS builder
ENV UV_COMPILE_BYTECODE=1 UV_LINK_MODE=copy
# Disable Python downloads, because we want to use the system interpreter
# across both images. If using a managed Python version, it needs to be
# copied from the build image into the final image; see `standalone.Dockerfile`
# for an example.
ENV UV_PYTHON_DOWNLOADS=0
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock \
    --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml \
    uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-dev
ADD . /app
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    uv sync --frozen --no-dev
###########################################
# PRODUCTION stage
###########################################
FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS production
# Copy the application from the builder
COPY --from=builder --chown=app:app /app /app
# Production-specific env
ENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"
# Default prod command (no reload)
CMD ["uvicorn", "src.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

We are using this multistage Dockerfile specifically because it is the most suitable for production. This is the my docker-compose.yamlfile:

## From the example docker-compose.yaml file provided by uv## https://github.com/astral-sh/uv-docker-example
## https://github.com/astral-sh/uv-docker-example/blob/main/compose.yml
  web:
    # Build the image from the Dockerfile in the current directory
    build:      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    # Host the FastAPI application on port 8000
    ports:      # Create a `watch` configuration to update the app
      # https://docs.docker.com/compose/file-watch/#compose-watch-versus-bind-mounts
      watch:
        # Sync the working directory with the `/app` directory in the container
        - action: rebuild          target: /app
          # Exclude the project virtual environment — it could be for a
          # different platform in the container            - .venv/
        # Rebuild the image if dependencies change by checking uv.lock
        - action: rebuild

Now, we can simply run docker compose up — watchand our service is running. We are using--watch so that when we make code changes, the container is rebuilt. This makes life easier as we do not have to manually shut down and rebuild our container to test code changes.

This wraps up the first of the implementation articles in this series. It may seem like a lot of work to make a very simply FastAPI service, but down the line when we extend functionality, it is going to be so much easier to add new features because we made the right structure from the get go. In the next article we are going to dive into Exceptions and Logging, to make our API robust and easy to monitor.

items.title: Introducing Docker Hardened Images: Secure, Minimal, and Ready for Production
items.author: Michael Donovan
items.content:
From the start, Docker has focused on enabling developers to build, share, and run software efficiently and securely. Today, Docker Hub powers software delivery at a global scale, with over 14 million images and more than 11 billion pulls each month. That scale gives us a unique vantage point into how modern software is built and the challenges teams face in securing it.

That’s why we’ve made security a cornerstone of our platform. From trusted Docker Official Images to SBOM support for transparency, the launch of Docker Scout for real-time vulnerability insights, and a hardened Docker Desktop to secure local development, every investment reflects our commitment to making software supply chain security more accessible, actionable, and developer-first.

Now, we’re taking that commitment even further.

We’re excited to introduce Docker Hardened Images (DHI) — secure-by-default container images purpose-built for modern production environments.

These images go far beyond being just slim or minimal. Docker Hardened Images start with a dramatically reduced attack surface, up to 95% smaller, to limit exposure from the outset. Each image is curated and maintained by Docker, kept continuously up to date to ensure near-zero known CVEs. They support widely adopted distros like Alpine and Debian, so teams can integrate them without retooling or compromising compatibility.

Plus, they’re designed to work seamlessly with the tools you already depend on. We’ve partnered with a range of leading security and DevOps platforms, including Microsoft, NGINX, Sonatype, GitLab, Wiz, Grype, Neo4j, JFrog, Sysdig and Cloudsmith, to ensure seamless integration with scanning tools, registries, and CI/CD pipelines.

What we’re hearing from customers

We talk to teams every day, from fast-moving startups to global enterprises, and the same themes keep coming up.

Integrity is a growing concern: “How do we know every component in our software is exactly what it claims to be—and hasn’t been tampered with?” With so many dependencies, it’s getting harder to answer that with confidence.

Then there’s the attack surface problem. Most teams start with general-purpose base images like Ubuntu or Alpine. But over time, these containers get bloated with unnecessary packages and outdated software, creating more ways in for attackers.

And of course, operational overhead is through the roof. Security teams are flooded with CVEs. Developers are stuck in a loop of patching and re-patching, instead of shipping new features. We’re hearing about vulnerability scanners lighting up constantly, platform teams stretched thin by centralized dependencies, and developers resorting to manual upgrades just to stay afloat. These challenges aren’t isolated — they’re systemic. And they’re exactly what we designed Docker Hardened Images to address.

Inside Docker Hardened Images

Docker Hardened Images aren’t just trimmed-down versions of existing containers — they’re built from the ground up with security, efficiency, and real-world usability in mind. They’re designed to meet teams where they are. Here’s how they deliver value across three essential areas:

First, they integrate seamlessly into existing workflows. Unlike other minimal or “secure” images that force teams to change base OSes, rewrite Dockerfiles, or abandon tooling, DHI supports the distributions developers already use, including familiar Debian and Alpine variants. In fact, upgrading to a DHI can be simple. Switching to a hardened image is as simple as updating one line in your Dockerfile:

Second, they strike the right balance between security and flexibility. Security shouldn’t mean sacrificing usability. DHI supports the customizations teams rely on, including certificates, packages, scripts, and configuration files, without compromising the hardened foundation. You get the security posture you need with the flexibility to tailor images to your environment.

Under the hood, Docker Hardened Images follow a distroless philosophy, stripping away unnecessary components like shells, package managers, and debugging tools that commonly introduce risk. While these extras might be helpful during development, they significantly expand the attack surface in production, slow down startup times, and complicate security management.

By including only the essential runtime dependencies needed to run your application, DHI delivers leaner, faster containers that are easier to secure and maintain. This focused, minimal design leads to up to a 95% reduction in attack surface, giving teams a dramatically stronger security posture right out of the box.

Automated Patching & Rapid CVE Response

Finally, patching and updates are continuous and automated. Docker monitors upstream sources, OS packages, and CVEs across all dependencies. When updates are released, DHI images are rebuilt, subjected to extensive testing, and published with fresh attestations—ensuring integrity and compliance within our SLSA Build Level 3–compliant build system. The result: you’re always running the most secure, verified version—no manual intervention required.

Most importantly, when essential components are built directly from source, allowing us to deliver critical patches faster and remediate vulnerabilities promptly. We patch Critical and High-severity CVEs within 7 days — faster than typical industry response times —and back it all with an enterprise-grade SLA for added peace of mind.

Internal Adoption: Validating Docker Hardened Images in Production Environments

We’ve been using DHI internally across several key projects — putting them to the test in real-world, production environments. One standout example is our internal use of a hardened Node image. 

By replacing the standard Node base image with a Docker Hardened Image, we saw immediate and measurable results: vulnerabilities dropped to zero, and the package count was reduced by over 98%. 

That reduction in packages isn’t just a matter of image size, it directly translates to a smaller attack surface, fewer moving parts to manage, and significantly less overhead for our security and platform teams. This shift gave us a stronger security posture and simplified operational complexity — exactly the kind of outcome we designed DHI to deliver.

Docker Hardened Images are designed to help you ship software with confidence by dramatically reducing your attack surface, automating patching, and integrating seamlessly into your existing workflows. Developers stay focused on building. Security teams get the assurance they need.

Looking to reduce your vulnerability count?
